title: CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita
subtitle: Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. [Fossil fuel and industry emissions](#dod:fossilemissions) are included. [Land-use change emissions](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) are not included.
caption:
CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita, 2024
Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. Fossil fuel and industry emissions are included. Land-use change emissions are not included.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between per capita energy derived from fossil fuels and per capita carbon dioxide emissions for countries in 2024. The horizontal axis measures energy consumption in kilowatt-hours per person, while the vertical axis measures CO₂ emissions in tonnes per person, with both scales plotted logarithmically. Circles sized by population distinguish the scale of nations, and color coding identifies income groups ranging from lower-middle-income to high-income countries. The data points form a tight, positive linear trend, showing that higher fossil fuel energy use coincides with higher carbon emissions across the global distribution. At the lower end of the spectrum, Pakistan and the Philippines sit in the bottom-left corner, with energy consumption below 5,000 kWh and emissions under 1 tonne per person. Moving toward the center, populous nations like India and Indonesia cluster between 5,000 and 10,000 kWh, with emissions ranging from roughly 2 to 3 tonnes. China and Japan occupy the middle ground with energy use near 30,000 kWh and emissions between 7 and 9 tonnes. High-income nations such as the United States, South Korea, and the Netherlands group further right, consuming over 50,000 kWh and emitting between 10 and 15 tonnes. At the extreme upper right, Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia display the highest values, with energy consumption exceeding 100,000 kWh and emissions surpassing 20 tonnes per person. The visual arrangement confirms a direct correspondence where increases in per capita fossil fuel energy align with proportional increases in per capita CO₂ output.
